items.evidence_text:
Client 2: Works.
Advisor: Starting with income—David, what do you think your combined gross household income is in a typical year? Ballpark is fine.
Client 1: Around two-twenty-ish. Like $222,550 a year gross, give or take.
Client 2: I would’ve said like $210k, but David tracks it closer than I do.
Advisor: Okay, so we’ll use about $222,550 gross household income annually. Patrici, does that feel like the right “before tax” number, not take-home?

Advisor: Great. And going back to what you said, David—cash flow feels heavy—let’s pin down monthly spending. About how much do you spend per month total, all-in?
Client 2: It’s like… sixteen thousand? Maybe seventeen?
Client 1: It’s basically $16,950 a month. That’s the number we landed on when we added it up.
Advisor: $16,950 per month total expenses—okay. When you say “expenses,” are you including debt payments in that, or is that separate in your mind?
Client 2: I was including the mortgage in that, yeah.

Client 1: I wasn’t. I mean—sorry—our spreadsheet has expenses and then debt payments separately.
Advisor: Perfect, that’s a common split. Let’s do it your spreadsheet way: total monthly expenses $16,950, and then we’ll also capture monthly debt costs separately. What are the debt payments per month across everything?
Client 1: Mortgage is $6,200 a month, and then that other loan is $500 a month. So $6,700 total monthly debt cost.
Client 2: The $500 one is the annoying one—high rate.
Advisor: Got it—$6,700 per month in debt payments. And just to make sure I’m not mixing terms: that $6,200 is the monthly payment, not the remaining balance, right?

Advisor: Good question—no, not the house. Just the accounts you can invest from: brokerage, retirement, bank/investment cash, private investments.
Client 2: Okay. Then… I don’t know, like nine hundred thousand?
Client 1: It’s $936,600 total investable assets.
Advisor: $936,600—thank you. And we’ll break it down in a minute. Patrici, you’re okay with that total?
Client 2: Yeah, that sounds right. I just don’t remember the exact total.

Client 1: Yeah, seriously. Anyway—go ahead.
Advisor: Alright—thanks. Going back to the mortgage for a second, David: we’ve got the mortgage balance at $142,950. When you think about your property value, what number do you use for the home these days?
Client 1: We use about $189,300 for the house value.
Client 2: I thought it was closer to two hundred—
Client 1: No, Patrici, that’s the old estimate. The one we’ve been using is $189,300.

Client 2: Okay, yes—value about $189,300, and we owe the $142,950 on the mortgage.
Advisor: Perfect. Now, you’ve got that other loan too—the one at 13.61%—and you said the balance is $2,850. When we add up everything you owe across debts, what’s the total outstanding you want me to use?
Client 1: Total outstanding across the mortgage plus that loan should be $145,800—well, $145,750.
Client 2: Isn’t it $146-something?
Client 1: No—$142,950 plus $2,850 is $145,800, but the total we’re using is $145,750. It’s basically that.

Advisor: Got it—$6,700 per month in debt payments. And just to make sure I’m not mixing terms: that $6,200 is the monthly payment, not the remaining balance, right?
Client 2: Yeah—payment. The balance is the big number.
Client 1: Right, payment is $6,200. Balance is around $143k on the mortgage.
Advisor: Thanks—hang on, I’m just writing that down. Now, assets: roughly how much do you have in investable assets—so brokerage, retirement accounts, cash-like accounts you invest from—excluding the house?
Client 2: Is that like… everything we own? Including the house value?

Advisor: Great. Now the other loan—the $500 a month one—what’s the interest rate on that?
Client 2: It’s awful—like thirteen percent.
Client 1: 13.61%. Balance is $2,850.
Advisor: Okay—13.61% interest, $2,850 outstanding, $500 a month. And when does that one end if you just keep paying as scheduled?
Client 1: January 2032—1/3/2032, I believe.

Advisor: Hi David, hi Patrici—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into numbers, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Hey Alex. Mostly just… we’re doing fine, but I want a tighter plan. Cash flow feels heavy.
Client 2: Hi. Yeah, and I want to make sure we’re not missing anything obvious—like insurance or debt stuff.
Advisor: Totally fair. We’ll go step by step. Quick framing: I’ll ask about income, then assets, then debts and protection—sound okay?

Advisor: Okay. Now, last admin-y thing: what tax bracket do you think you’re landing in these days? Like the marginal bracket.
Client 2: Uh—35? Or is that like… our average? I always mix that up.
Advisor: Totally normal. Marginal is the top bracket your next dollar falls into, not your average rate. David, does 35% sound right for marginal?
Client 1: Yes—35% marginal is what we’ve been using.
Client 2: Okay, so 35% is the “next dollar” one. Got it.

Client 2: No, none. It’s just us.
Client 1: Yeah, zero dependents.
Advisor: Got it—no dependents. And you’re married, right? I don’t want to assume—married versus domestic partnership, that kind of thing.
Client 2: Married.
Client 1: Yep, married.

Advisor: Hi David, hi Patrici—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into numbers, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Hey Alex. Mostly just… we’re doing fine, but I want a tighter plan. Cash flow feels heavy.
Client 2: Hi. Yeah, and I want to make sure we’re not missing anything obvious—like insurance or debt stuff.
Advisor: Totally fair. We’ll go step by step. Quick framing: I’ll ask about income, then assets, then debts and protection—sound okay?

Client 1: Correct, I’m Client 1.
Client 2: And I’m Client 2, yes.
Advisor: Perfect. Now, zooming out a bit—when we talk about your overall investing comfort level, would you say you’re conservative, moderate, or aggressive? Like, how much market up-and-down can you tolerate without bailing at the worst time?
Client 2: I mean, I’m probably conservative? I don’t like big drops.
Client 1: I’d say we’re moderate. She’s cautious, but we’re not all-cash or anything.

Advisor: Hi David, hi Patrici—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into numbers, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Hey Alex. Mostly just… we’re doing fine, but I want a tighter plan. Cash flow feels heavy.
Client 2: Hi. Yeah, and I want to make sure we’re not missing anything obvious—like insurance or debt stuff.
Advisor: Totally fair. We’ll go step by step. Quick framing: I’ll ask about income, then assets, then debts and protection—sound okay?
Client 1: Yep.

Client 2: Yeah, moderate.
Advisor: Good. And just so I don’t assume—when you say “surprises,” Patrici, you mean like the account value swinging, not like the monthly mortgage payment changing, right?
Client 2: Wait, isn’t the mortgage payment kind of… variable? Like it can go up?
Client 1: No—ours is fixed. The escrow can move a bit, but the rate’s fixed.
Advisor: Right—thank you, David. I should’ve been clearer: the interest rate is fixed, but taxes/insurance can nudge the payment. Your mortgage rate was 3.17%, and you said the payment is about $6,200 a month—still the ballpark?

Client 1: I wasn’t. I mean—sorry—our spreadsheet has expenses and then debt payments separately.
Advisor: Perfect, that’s a common split. Let’s do it your spreadsheet way: total monthly expenses $16,950, and then we’ll also capture monthly debt costs separately. What are the debt payments per month across everything?
Client 1: Mortgage is $6,200 a month, and then that other loan is $500 a month. So $6,700 total monthly debt cost.
Client 2: The $500 one is the annoying one—high rate.
Advisor: Got it—$6,700 per month in debt payments. And just to make sure I’m not mixing terms: that $6,200 is the monthly payment, not the remaining balance, right?

Advisor: Perfect. And roughly what’s your gross annual income from that job, Patrici—before taxes and benefits?
Client 2: Um… I think it’s like sixty-five-ish? Around there.
Client 1: It’s not 65. It’s $59,400 gross—she just got used to rounding it up.
Client 2: Okay—yeah, sorry. $59,400 is the actual gross.
Advisor: Got it—$59,400 gross for you, Patrici. Thanks for tightening that up.

Advisor: Hi David, hi Patrici—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into numbers, what prompted you to set this up now?

Advisor: Alright. Going back to what you said earlier about wanting fewer surprises, Patrici—let’s zoom in on your paycheck for a second, because that’s usually the steady anchor.
Advisor: Patrici, are you currently employed, like W-2 employed somewhere, versus contract work?
Client 2: Yeah, I’m employed—regular job, not contract.
Client 1: W-2, yep.

Client 1: Correct—Client 1 is me, Client 2 is Patrici.
Advisor: Perfect. Sorry—one more admin-y thing while we’re on you, Patrici: your date of birth. Month, day, year?
Client 2: February 1st, 1977.
Advisor: February 1st, 1977—thank you.
Client 1: Yeah, that’s right.

Client 2: No, none. It’s just us.
Client 1: Yeah, zero dependents.

Client 1: Yep, married.

Client 1: Yeah, spouse/partner is right.
Advisor: Perfect. And Patrici, what kind of work do you do—like broadly? I’m not looking for your employer, just the category.
Client 2: Professional—office-type work. Not like trades or anything.
Client 1: Yeah, she’s in a professional role.
Advisor: Got it. Now, David—switching back to you—what’s your gross annual income from your work? Just the main earned income number.

Client 1: It’s about $160k—
Client 2: No, it’s higher than that—
Client 1: —hang on, you’re right to push me. The actual gross is $163,150 a year.
Advisor: Thank you. And David, when you say “gross,” you mean before taxes and any deductions—same definition we just used with Patrici?
Client 1: Yes, gross before taxes.

Client 1: Right—hers is stable, mine’s more variable month to month.

Client 1: Yes, I’m self-employed.
Client 2: He is, yeah.

Client 2: I mean—he does a lot of ops stuff, but it’s also—

Client 1: Yes, that’s right.
Client 2: Yeah.

Client 2: We’re not in one of the big ones you always hear about. It’s… just other.
Client 1: Yeah, not California or New York—just “other.”

Client 1: Yep.
Advisor: Okay. Now the last one is a little admin-y, but important: David, what’s your date of birth?
Client 1: Uh, February 19th, 1983.

Client 1: No—19th. I’m sure.

Client 1: I think it’s like seventy-five thousand a year? Somewhere in there.
Client 2: I thought it was closer to eighty.
Client 1: No, hang on—let me think. It’s $6,350 a month, so annual is $76,200.
Advisor: One sec—writing that down. So $76,200 per year, coming in monthly, and it’s David’s Social Security line.
Client 2: When you say “per year,” that’s gross, right? Not like what lands after taxes?

Advisor: Yep—gross is before taxes and deductions. Net is what hits your checking account.
Client 2: Got it. Then yeah, gross around $222,550 sounds right.

Client 2: It’s like… sixteen thousand? Maybe seventeen?
